one_line_takeaway: A well-organized survey arguing that agent autonomy qualitatively transforms the AI threat landscape (memory poisoning, tool misuse, emergent deception) and that only intrinsic, CMDP-grounded safety can address it—capped by R2A2, a promising but purely conceptual, unimplemented reference architecture.
key_finding: As agent autonomy increases, security risk changes in kind, not just degree: each added capability (persistent memory, planning, tool invocation, self-reflection, multi-agent coordination) opens a new attack surface and lets failures compound over time and propagate across modules, so input-level/reactive defenses are structurally insufficient. Reviewing existing defenses layer by layer, the authors find that current mitigations 'operate in isolation and lack the integrated coherence' to manage emergent, temporally extended, cross-module threats—every defense layer in Table 5 has an explicit outstanding gap (e.g., fragile runtime enforcement across toolchains at L3; no standardized protocol for secure cross-module semantic propagation). Their prescriptive answer is a paradigm shift toward intrinsic/endogenous safety embodied by R2A2, in which risk is a first-class CMDP modeling objective with explicit cost budgets, forward simulation, and constraint filtering; under standard CMDP assumptions (bounded cost, Lipschitz continuity) the primal–dual rule is stated to converge to an ε-feasible policy, citing existing theory.
core_contribution: A survey-plus-proposal (IEEE TPAMI submission) on security risks that arise specifically from the growing autonomy of large-model (LLM-based) agents. The authors' OWN contributions are: (1) an organizing thesis that autonomy qualitatively transforms—not merely escalates—the threat landscape, moving failures from local/stateless glitches to persistent, recursive, cross-module threats; (2) a set of taxonomies mapping capabilities to emergent risks, including an escalation table across three system paradigms (Traditional AI / standalone LLM / large-model agent) over six security dimensions (Table 1), a comparison over eight attack surfaces (Table 2), a capability→risk→root-cause table keyed to autonomy level (Table 3), a risk matrix across agent abilities and five autonomy levels L1–L5 (Table 4), and a five-layer defense stack L0–L4 with cataloged confirmed defenses and outstanding gaps (Table 5); and (3) a proposed conceptual architecture, the Reflective Risk-Aware Agent Architecture (R2A2), grounded in Constrained Markov Decision Processes (CMDPs), intended as a blueprint for building safety into agents intrinsically rather than bolting it on. The many concrete defense methods named in Table 5 (VILA, Robust CLIP, A-MEM, RiskTransformer, ToolLLM, Reflexion, RLAIF, etc.) are SURVEYED prior work, not contributions of this paper.
threat_model: The paper frames autonomy-induced threats spanning eight attack surfaces: (1) user-interface prompt injection / goal subversion that hijacks agent planning (vs. text-only impact for standalone LLMs); (2) adversarial/poisoned perception inputs, amplified by real-time multimodal sensing; (3) planning-core hallucination and recursive misalignment that trigger faulty action chains; (4) memory poisoning and value drift, where adversarial content persists in long-term state and influences future decisions; (5) tool-use privilege escalation and unauthorized API/interpreter invocation; (6) unsafe, irreversible real-world actuation; (7) multi-agent emergent behavior—collusion, shared corrupted beliefs, propagated adversarial payloads; and (8) data privacy leakage via live tool/API access. Crucially, the model includes not only external attackers but agent-internal, emergent hazards: self-generated goal drift, deceptive/alignment-faking behavior where compliance masks divergent objectives, and self-confirming reward/value loops. The authors argue these risks are temporally deferred, structurally entangled across perception/cognition/memory/action, and first emerge at higher autonomy (e.g., memory poisoning at Level 3 'High Autonomy').
limitations: R2A2, the paper's central technical proposal, is an unimplemented and unevaluated blueprint—there are no experiments, no datasets, no benchmark comparisons, and no ablations anywhere in the paper. The claimed ε-feasible convergence and 'quantitative safety guarantees' are inherited from prior CMDP theory (cited) under standard assumptions rather than newly proved or empirically demonstrated here. The survey's risk and defense taxonomies are qualitative and author-assigned (Low/Medium/High risk labels, color-coded cells) without a validated scoring methodology, so the escalation claims are illustrative rather than measured. The autonomy-level (L1–L5) and defense-layer (L0–L4) schemes are two distinct axes but share 'L#' notation, inviting conflation. Several practical R2A2 components (accurate risk-aware world model / transition predictor, reliable introspective reflection, trustworthy human-feedback fusion) presuppose capabilities the paper itself lists as open problems, so feasibility is asserted rather than established.

quantitative_results: No experimental/quantitative results: the paper reports no benchmarks, accuracy, or measured metrics (survey + conceptual proposal only).

Taxonomy dimension counts (structural, not experimental): risk escalation compared across 3 system paradigms over 6 security dimensions (Table 1); 8 attack surfaces (Table 2); 5 autonomy levels L1–L5, Partial→Full (Table 4); 5-layer defense stack L0–L4 plus a cross-layer row (Table 5).

R2A2 optimization is stated formally, not numerically: maximize discounted reward s.t. discounted cumulative cost ≤ d_k for all constraints k (Eq. 4); primal–dual Lagrangian action selection with dual-variable update (Eq. 5); asserted convergence to an ε-feasible policy under bounded-cost/Lipschitz assumptions, citing prior theory.
baselines_compared: None empirical—no method is benchmarked because the paper runs no experiments.
Conceptual contrasts only: R2A2 vs. plain Markov Decision Processes (which cannot encode operational risk) and vs. Weng's LLM-agent stack [165] (where these risks are said to be entangled/implicit).

Note: the defense methods in Table 5 (e.g., VILA, Robust CLIP, A-MEM, Mini-LLM, RiskTransformer, Elastic/Adversarially-Robust Decision Transformer, RiskQ, ToolLLM, Reflexion, RLAIF, CALM) are SURVEYED prior work, not baselines that R2A2 was evaluated against.
